How does a natural monopoly differ from a legal‚Äã monopoly?
Question content area bottom
Part 1
A natural monopoly is a market in which‚Äã _______. A legal monopoly is a market in which‚Äã _______.
A.
demand is perfectly elastic due to economies of‚Äã scale;
many producers of similar products lobby the government to restrict entry to new firms
B.
economies of scale enable one firm to supply the entire market at the lowest possible‚Äã cost;
competition and entry are restricted by the granting of a public‚Äã franchise, government‚Äã license, patent, or copyright
C.
close substitutes exist but a natural barrier to entry protects the firm from potential‚Äã competition;
a firm could practise price discrimination but is unable to do so due to the granting of a public‚Äã franchise, government‚Äã license, patent, or copyright
D.
a significant portion of a natural resource is owned by one‚Äã firm;
competition has been deemed to be legal and in the best interest of consumers

How does a‚Äã single-price monopoly determine the price it will charge its‚Äã customers?
Question content area bottom left
Part 1
A‚Äã single-price monopoly‚Äã _______.
A.
charges the price from the demand curve that corresponds to the quantity where the price elasticity of demand equals 1
B.
produces the quantity at which marginal revenue equals marginal cost and sets the price equal to marginal revenue at that quantity
C.
produces the quantity at which marginal revenue equals marginal cost and charges the highest price consumers will pay for that quantity from the demand curve
D.
produces the quantity at which average total cost is minimized and charges the highest price consumers will pay for that quantity from the demand curve

B.
produces the quantity at which marginal revenue equals marginal cost and sets the price equal to marginal revenue at that quantity

When a monopoly produces the‚Äã profit-maximizing quantity,‚Äã _______.
A.
price is greater than marginal‚Äã revenue, which is greater than marginal cost
B.
marginal revenue equals marginal‚Äã cost, and price is greater than marginal revenue.
C.
marginal cost is‚Äã minimized, and marginal revenue equals price
D.
marginal‚Äã revenue, marginal‚Äã cost, and price are all equal

D.
marginal‚Äã revenue, marginal‚Äã cost, and price are all equal

Why can a monopoly make a positive economic profit even in the long‚Äã run?
Question content area bottom
Part 1
A monopoly can make positive economic profit in the long run because‚Äã _______.
A.
barriers to entry prevent other firms from entering the market and sharing the profit
B.
eventually demand will increase and prices will rise
C.
new technology constantly lowers costs for the monopoly firm and for its competitors
D.
other firms eventually exit the market until the one remaining firm is making an economic profit

A.
barriers to entry prevent other firms from entering the market and sharing the profit

If marginal revenue is positive at the actual number of surfboard rentals made each‚Äã hour, then‚Äã _______.
A.
the demand for surfboard rentals is inelastic
B.
‚ÄãSam's Surfboards can increase economic profit by increasing the number of rentals‚ÄÉ‚ÄÉ‚ÄÉ
C.
the demand for surfboard rentals is unit elastic
D.
the demand for surfboard rentals is elastic

D.
the demand for surfboard rentals is elastic

Does a monopoly produce on the elastic or inelastic range of the market demand‚Äã curve?

A monopoly‚Äã _______.
A.
never produces an output in the elastic range of the market demand because it could charge a higher‚Äã price, produce a smaller‚Äã quantity, and increase its profit
B.
usually produces the quantity at which market demand is unit‚Äã elastic, and at that quantity total revenue is maximized
C.
always produces output in the inelastic range of the market demand
D.
never produces an output in the inelastic range of the market demand because it could charge a higher‚Äã price, produce a smaller‚Äã quantity, and increase its profit

D.
never produces an output in the inelastic range of the market demand because it could charge a higher‚Äã price, produce a smaller‚Äã quantity, and increase its profit

Why is a‚Äã single-price monopoly‚Äã inefficient?
Question content area bottom
Part 1
A‚Äã single-price monopoly is inefficient because‚Äã _______ at the quantity produced.
A.
marginal social benefit is maximized
B.
marginal social cost is minimized
C.
marginal social cost exceeds marginal social benefit
D.
marginal social benefit exceeds marginal social cost

C.
marginal social cost exceeds marginal social benefit

When new wells are discovered and‚Äã Minnie's faces competition from new‚Äã producers, ‚Äã _______.
A.
the market supply of water increases and the market price‚Äã falls, so‚Äã Minnie's produces more output so that at the lower price her economic profit remains unchanged
B.
the market demand for water increases and the market price‚Äã rises, so‚Äã Minnie's produces more‚Äã output, charges a higher‚Äã price, and makes a larger economic profit
C.
the market supply of water increases and the market price‚Äã falls, so‚Äã Minnie's produces less‚Äã output, charges a lower‚Äã price, and makes a smaller economic profit
D.
Minnie makes no changes because she is already maximizing economic profit
C. the market supply of water increases and the market price‚Äã falls, so‚Äã Minnie's produces less‚Äã output, charges a lower‚Äã price, and makes a smaller economic profit

How are the curves identified when a perfectly competitive industry is taken over by a single‚Äã firm?
The competitive‚Äã industry's _______ curve becomes the‚Äã monopoly's _______ curve.
A.
average total‚Äã cost; supply
B.
‚Äãsupply; marginal cost 
C.
average total‚Äã cost; marginal cost
D.
marginal‚Äã revenue; demand

B.
‚Äãsupply; marginal cost
